sentences.text: So I-- so they came at night, the KG-- the NKVD.
There And they said, we give you one hour.
You should pack.
But one of them said nicely, make sure to take warm clothes.
We didn't know what and how.
And they stood with us.
And they-- it wasn't-- didn't even give us an hour.
They stood with us, took us right away to the station, and they loaded us with cattle wagons.
And there were a lot of people there already.
And the wagons were closed.
There was a, like, a toilet, a hole.
And there's a lot of people, with strangers.
And to me, this was again-- I said, where are we going now?
Like, always my hope is-- my hope was, maybe I'm going to go home.
Maybe I'm going to go home.
And here, I say, I wouldn't go home anymore.
But we didn't know where they are taking us.
We were loaded there till the-- each wagon was really in capacity.
And there was-- and they locked the wagons.
And we were traveling.
They brought us-- once a day, they brought some soup and bread.
And we were traveling like this for a few days.
And then they opened-- when we were already in Russia, they opened the wagons.
And then there was a little bit of fresh air.
And they opened the wagons.
And we were traveling with those wagons-- in the wagons probably with the train maybe, like, three weeks.
I really don't remember.
And then, from the wagons, we went on the barge.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were traveling with the barge.
And on the barge, I got-- no, we were traveling about four weeks because we went till the end.
There were-- they didn't go any more.
The trains didn't go anymore.
This was already beginning of Siberia, where the trains didn't go, after [INAUDIBLE].
And so we went already with the barge with the water.
And on the barge, I got sick.
I got sick.
And I don't remember-- I don't know how many days we were.
But I, I think I was a few days on the barge.
And on the barge was already, at this time-- the hygienic thing.
There was lice all over.
Everybody had lice.
Everybody had lice.
It was terrible with lice.
And when we're traveling, maybe, like, 8, 10 days with barge, I got sick.
I got sick.
And I went in coma.
But I didn't remember.
But I remember one thing which was in my memory-- then they were carrying me.
And somebody asked, who is this?
So they said, it's Sally.
So the other person was, she's dead.
So the person said, most probably.
And I still remember how happy I was.
I thought I am dead.
And I hear this after the death.
And I was so happy that I am dead.
I still feel this wonderful feeling.
Oh, I am dead.
It's finished.
But I went back in coma.
And they did take me yet with, with the trucks.
But I was already at this time incoherent, you know, like, out of coma, in coma.
And finally, the next which I found myself, I found myself in a place-- in a-- around in a taiga, which is tundra.
What I got was a shed.
And quite a few people was there in the shed.
And all are covered with white sheets.
And I thought that I died, and I'm in heaven.
And they were talking Russian.
I didn't understand Russian.
So I said, well, they probably talk-- that's why I don't understand what they are talking because I'm in heaven.
And I was going in coma and out of coma.
I mean, you don't-- and then again, I didn't know what happened till finally I got better.
And then I found out that I was left.
I found out later that my uncle was putting up a terrible fight [CRYING] not to leave me.
[SOBBING] But they didn't.
They just left me alone.
And this I will never forget-- when I came completely to consciousness and I found myself there, all alone.
I didn't understand the language.
I didn't know happened to me.
This feeling-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till I die [SOBBING] I will remember.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then took-- I don't really remember the time because I don't know how long.
I got a little bit better, stronger.
They-- there was no medicine there.
There were no doctor.
There was just a felcher.
A felcher is like a doctor's associate, you know, like-- but no doctor.
There were, like, to-- and people every single day were dying.
They-- this was called [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, people-- they brought there people with typhoid.
Would they-- you die, finished.
Every single day, few people died.
And they took them out from the room.
And then, when I was a little bit better and I got off the bed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw there a, a woman.
And I saw that somebody is coming to her.
A man is coming to her, but they didn't let him in-- but through the window.
And this woman was talking with the brother Jewish.
I, I didn't speak too well Jewish, but I spoke Jewish.
And I-- so I said, what?
This is somebody from us.
So I went over to her.
Her name was-- oh, I don't-- after the marriage, she was Horowitz.
But I don't remember.
Anyhow, she told me, yes, I got sick also.
But I'm from different transport.
And my brother--
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [DRINKS] --my brother's here with me.
And to me, this was such a wonderful thing.
Somebody's here-- which I could communicate.
And somebody which is Jewish, somebody who is from our people.

And-- but her transport came out, was very close-- yes, the place which, which this-- what-- which this-- what you-- they called [NON-ENGLISH] where the [NON-ENGLISH]-- with the shack-- was plain shack.
It's called Zaiarsk, Zaiarsk.
And she told me that her transport is [NON-ENGLISH].
They are in [NON-ENGLISH], which is not far away from Zaiarsk.
And her brother comes here to her.
They'd-- they-- he got permission he would let her [INAUDIBLE].
But she got better faster.
So she go.
She was probably stronger-- older, she was much older than I was.
And she went back to [NON-ENGLISH].
And here I was still very weak.
And then they came again.
They probably called the NKVD.
He came.
And for some reason, I don't know why, I said they're my parents, and I was with my parents.
I get-- I think where I got this and how I-- why I did it, until today, I don't know.
But instead of saying that I'm with my uncle, I said I'm with my parents.
Because I figure maybe if they are going to save my parents, they will send me.
If I am going to say with the uncle, they're going to leave me here.
And my-- I didn't care if I'm there [INAUDIBLE] everything.
But I wanted [SOBBING] that I should go home to my parents.
And then they-- this was already end of September because it was-- the water was freezing already.
And they put me on the boat alone.
And that was the last boat.
When we were going, the water was half-frozen already.
The boat hardly could go because September there is already all-- and the boat came.
On the boat, I had no food, no place.
But the Russian people were very good.
Whoever ate, somebody left me a little bit to eat.
[SOBBING] They were nice.
[SOBBING] I guess they have pity me.
My head were shaved.
I, I didn't have shoes.
Because when I was left, I was left without shoes.
Probably what they think, that they took me off.
Because I understand they promised him to the last time the-- because the whole time the NKVD was with us.
The NKVD promised that they're going to put me on their-- whatever they-- whatever they go with.
And the last minute, they didn't.
They just send me.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so there, you know, there on the boat, the Russian people got pity on me.
And they gave me food.
I was sleeping in there-- outside, though it was very cold.
So I went inside.
They let me sleep, you know, where the workers were sleeping, and came to Iakutsk-- came to Iakutsk.
And I'm coming [INAUDIBLE].
The captain from the boat calls the NKVD, what to do with me?
NKVD comes, takes me, puts me in the orphan for prisoners-- prisoners children.
Well, I was so frightened.
I mean, everybody had shaved hair there.
But my hair was shaved anyhow, so they didn't have to shave again.
And I still say, I want-- I-- my parents are there.
I want to know where my parents-- I want to go my parents, I say to the NKVD.
So he's laughing at me.
He says, what do you think?
Do you know how many people are here in the-- do you know where you are?
Who is going to know where your parents are?
Nobody knows where your parents are there.
But I kept on saying, my parents.
In this orphaned home, there was such tremendous hunger.
There was nothing to eat.
And whatever there was a drop of bread, they would-- the people which were working, they took it for themselves.
There was one there-- one woman, which was very kind and very good.
She really-- she never took anything.
But later, I found out why.
And I learned and-- by-- if you are 12-- if you are till 12 or 13, I don't remember, you, you could work with the younger children, and you get a double portion of bread.
So I said, I am 13.
Because-- so I worked.
I worked, but I learned in school.
I mean, I had lessons there, too-- Russian.
And and I quickly learned the language because this was the only one language what-- that was spoken to me.
And as a child, you learn.
So-- and so I learned.
So because I worked, I got a double portion of bread.
So that means instead of one slice a day, I got two slices a day because I worked.
I was there.
And they used to come at night, wake me up in the cold, and used to take me on the NKVD.
And they promised me they're going to send me back closer, you know, they're going to send me to Irkutsk, which is also in Siberia, but not so deep in Siberia.
Because this is-- this was already closer to Alaska.
So he says-- so they told me if I'm going to sign that I want to stay here, they're going to send me to school.
And I-- and they used to put food on the table.
They never gave me.
And they say, you see, you going to-- you won't be hungry.
You're going to have food.
And I said, no.
I don't want it.
I want to go to my parents.
Because I was afraid [SOBBING] that I will never come out from there.
And, and they used to take me [SOBBING] at night, always at night to wake me up, and take me on that-- once, I said, why are you waking me at night?
Why don't you take me daytime?
So he-- and I will never forget.
He says, NKVD [SPEAKING RUSSIAN].
It means, the NKVD works only at night.
And they used to take me every, every-- either twice a week or once a week.
And I was hungry.
I was tired.
But somehow, [SOBBING] I was strong enough to say, no, I'm not going.
I want to go to my parents.
You find my parents.
And here, I used to cry myself to sleep, [SOBBING] thinking, I will never see my parents.
I will never see anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And once a woman said to me a few words in Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I understood Jewish?
I said, yes, I do.
And so she told me, Sunday, come on this and this address.
Because Sunday, we were allowed to-- and she said to her-- listen, she's going to come to me Sunday, to the [NON-ENGLISH] which was there.
She's going to come to me Sunday to visit me Sunday.
Let her go.
I said, fine.
She gave me the address.
I'm coming there.
And I'm going-- this is the [NON-ENGLISH] NKVD, which means the head from the NKVD.
His name was [?
Nikrosov. ?] He-- I don't know.
He-- somehow he was not Jewish.
If she was married to a non-Jew and he was already a child from-- because he was not Jewish, but the mother was.
And I will never forget there was there food.
Because the whole-- everybody was hungry.
But there was so much food.
And she-- they wouldn't let me eat.
She says, don't worry.
You're going to have food.
You're going to come next week again.
But don't-- you-- but I couldn't stop, you know.
Of course I grabbed the food.
I grabbed the food, and I got very sick.
When I went-- but she told me I'm not allowed to say anybody which I eat here, which I see here.
And in the beginning, I was very frightened because when I saw that he's the [NON-ENGLISH] NKVD [?
Nikrosov, ?] so I was even more-- I was so terrified.
I mean, what's going to happen now?
She told me, don't be afraid.
He's-- so-- after I ate as much-- she didn't let me eat too much.
But even a little, which I ate-- so I got very sick.
And I went back there.
And I was sick for about four days, extremely sick, with the diarrhea, with vomiting-- extremely, extremely sick.
I thought that I'm dying.
That's how sick I was.
And the next Sunday, she told me I should come again.
And from this on, I was going there every Sunday.
And this I remember-- when came Friday, I took my two slices of bread, and I divided between the children.
Friday and Saturday I didn't eat.
I gave my bread to the little children because I knew that Sunday I will eat.
And Sunday-- I would eat already for a-- we would eat enough.
I mean, look, you, you didn't-- everything you were dreaming about is bread.
You weren't dreaming about food.
You were dreaming about bread, to have enough bread to eat.
So I knew that Sunday, I will eat already enough.
So I gave my-- every Friday and, and, and Saturday, I gave away my bread.
I mean, I-- maybe I took a drop from it.
I shouldn't be completely-- but I was going hungry but with that thought, tomorrow I'm gonna to eat.
And that thought, tomorrow I'm going to eat, kept me then.
I could be hungry.
And one day, he took the name of my-- which I told him also the name of my parents.
And [? across ?] of one Sunday, I come there.
And [? across ?] of concern, this, you know, with his guns, with his everything.
And he says, I found your parents.
They are in Minor.
And they also are looking.
Because he got in touch with Miroshnichenko, which was the NKVD, which was in the-- called the [NON-ENGLISH].
It means like a ghetto, which-- like a prison, you know.
But it was-- they builded themselves sheds, and they worked there.
So-- and there was always NKVD with them.
So, he says, your family was inquired by Miroshnichenko what could be done.
And they are there in there.
So I start to beg him.
I said, I want to go there.
I want to go there.
So he told me, listen, he says, you think you-- it's bad here, it's going to be much worse there.
You don't know how bad it's going to be there.
So I said, I don't care.
I don't care.
Just I want to go.
So he said, all right.
I will send you.
And he gave a, a NKVD along with me on the sleds.
We were traveling-- this was at least, I don't know, 1,000 kilometers or maybe even 2,000 kilometers from, from Iakutsk to, to Minor.
We stopped in a place, which called [NON-ENGLISH].
We stopped in a place called [NON-ENGLISH].
In [NON-ENGLISH], there were already some people from a different transport which were working, which they had like-- and they were working for the-- for the government.
They'd they let them work for the government.
And I stayed with those people for a few days.
And then he told me, would you like to stay here if your parents going to come?
Because it was very close to-- so I said, if they give them permission to come here, I would be very happy.
And then they-- and then they did give them permission.
And they came.
And they came to [NON-ENGLISH].
And [NON-ENGLISH] was already much better than there by them on Minor.
Because there wasn't food, but was at least something, you know.
You could get a card for something because you were working already.
And we were in [NON-ENGLISH] for a short time.
And then they decided again that we are against the government.
They sent back-- there were only a few people there-- back to Minor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then on Minor, we were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, Minor was a taiga.
It's a tundra, which was a [NON-ENGLISH], a close place, which were really sheds there, where every-- you know, like, 10 families builded themselves one shed.
And then they put it water.
And it froze like ice and snow so kept it warm, like an igloo.
And people had to work on the gold.
They had there-- they were digging gold.
They-- people-- everybody had to work on gold.
Or they had to work on that tundra to cut the tundra.
This was forcible work for everybody.
And of course, the-- there were no food.
There you got the ration, a small piece of bread for the day.
And you were trying-- the water-- you used the snow for the water.
And Minor, we were there till about 1943, I think, when, when they maked a pact with Poland.
When Russia made already a pact with Poland, I think there was-- and, at this time, they made us-- they told us we are free.
So they-- we are free.
They closed-- they opened-- they untied our hands.
But they untied our feet.
Where could you go from there?
There is no transportation.
One transportation is on our dogs or on elks, with the-- and there is-- where you could go there?
There is [LAUGHS] no place to go.
That's only taiga there on the tundra.
But after a while, they made another-- some agreement with the Polish government in exile.
Because we were considered Polish citizens.
And they-- and they send us back to Kolkhoz [NON-ENGLISH] 97.
There we were till the-- till-- from-- this must have been '44 when they send us.
I don't-- but I-- must be because we were there for a while.
In '45, we had the war.
But there was no radio there.
There was the one radio which broke.
So we didn't know that the war ended.
So two days later, or that we fixed the-- they fixed the radio.
And we heard the war ended, that it's end of the war.
And I remember we celebrated at this time the end of the war.
[SOBBING] At this time, I started to dream.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] I forget all my suffering.
[CRYING] I want to say it-- something else, that all the time, [SOBBING] I was extremely angry with my parents.
And for this, [SOBBING] I suffer until today.
Because [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I was so angry at them that I said, when I come back, I wouldn't talk to them.
I really didn't realize till I was mother myself what courage it took to send me away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] But still, I was dreaming.
[CRYING] And all this time, whatever had happened, it's-- to me, was like this, well, it's another day.
It's closer.
It's closer.
All those years, I figure, all right, we're past another day.
To me, this was not-- whatever happened, I didn't care what-- the whole life was only another day closer, closer to the time.
The war won't be forever.
I'm going to come home.
And I was even saying, like, I'm going to come home.
Like, I won't talk to them.
Because that's how angry I was.
And then we started to find out what happened.
I still didn't want to believe.
I, I had hopes.
I was dreaming, you know, not my parents.
I start to write letters home.
I start to write letters home to my friends from before the war.
I remember a friend, [?
Staszko. ?] Matter of fact, his son is still alive.
He's in Cracow.
And I wrote a letter to his family.
I wrote to [?
Kanski ?] because Janka [?
Kanski, ?] she was my friend.
I wrote to them letters.
Could you tell me what happened?
Where are my parents?
Where could I find them?
Of course, the letters were never answered.
But a cousin of mine, which was in Dabrowa, she was-- she went-- after the liberation, she went to Dabrowa.
And she-- and she-- they got-- they gave it to her.
They knew that she is my cousin.
So they gave her-- she was my second cousin.
They gave her the letters.
And she, she also didn't want-- first thing, the mail didn't go away, you know, could take about half a year if came already something.
And besides, she said, she would never-- she couldn't answer those letters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we were-- I was the-- '45 beginning-- end of '45, they sent us back.
Again-- we went again on the cattle wagons.
But they were-- we were freed.
And we were traveling.
We came to Poland.
And met very Jews.
And we were-- we knew already what happened.
We knew already about the tragedy.
We knew already-- but somehow I didn't believe it.
I didn't believe it.
I said, somehow maybe my parents survived, maybe.
I was going with so much hope that I'm going to come home.
We came to Poland.
We traveled over the Poland.
I also found out at this time that somebody took out from our house a lot of money.
And the funny thing is, when they told me that this-- that those two girls took out, I knew exactly the place where it was.
Because I remember before the war, we used to have such a tile-- big tiles ovens, such high-tile ovens.
And I remember they-- my father was doing something near the oven in the wall with somebody.
So I asked him what he's doing.
So he says, in case if it's a war, the, the-- they would throw gas, the gas shouldn't come in.
But when, listen, this was already six years later.
So when they told me that somebody took out something, I says, I'm going to tell you exactly which place this will be.
But this time, I realized that this wasn't for gas.
This was they-- where they put away.
Of course, I never got anything from them.
But that's a different thing.
And, and we were traveling till we came to the town Wroclaw.
Came the time Wroclaw, I went way out and went to the shul to find out something.
There were some people.
And they told us, if you want, there is apartment-- a German apartment here you could go in.
And I went, and I said to the wagon, we have an apartment.
And all the people which were on the wagon-- there were maybe a hundred, for sure-- we all went to this apartment.
I mean, one slept on top of the other, but didn't matter.
We all went to this apartment.
And this was already Poland.
Then It was Wroclaw.
This was Breslau originally, but that was Wroclaw.
And slowly people started to take-- they got themselves different places.
And everybody-- and we remained in this apartment.
And all of the families found also place to live in.
And, of course, in Wroclaw, we were thinking what to-- where-- to go away from Poland.
There were a few pogroms.
There were the Kielce pogrom.
There was in Cracow a pogrom.
And there was in Wroclaw a pogrom.
And I joined a-- that was called the Kibbutz Mizrahi, was-- were with my friends.
And this-- we supposed to go to Israel.
